text:
Admission Date: 1974-05-13        Discharge Date: 1985-04-25

Date of Birth:  2018-07-24        Sex:  M

Service:  NSU


HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 71 year old
gentleman with Stage III-B nonsmall cell carcinoma of the
lung, status post recent completion of chemotherapy/radiation
therapy, who presented on 1990-09-04, for preoperative elective
staging magnetic resonance imaging scan which showed a 3.0
centimeter right temporal lobe mass with surrounding edema
with two other small lesions also noted.  The patient was
sent to the Emergency Department for evaluation.

He was admitted on 1974-05-13, to the Hematology/Oncology
service.  The patient tolerated chemotherapy and radiation
therapy well with good shrinkage of the mediastinal lymph
nodes as well as the primary tumor within his left upper lobe
and was being preopped for a left upper lobectomy and radical
mediastinal lymph node dissection.  This is when the temporal
lobe lesion was discovered.  The patient was admitted to the
Oncology/Medicine service and then therefore transferred to
the Neurosurgery service for excision of this temporal lobe
lesion.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:  On physical examination, he was in no
acute distress.  He was awake, alert and oriented times
three.  His heart was regular rate and rhythm.  Lungs were
clear to auscultation bilaterally.  The abdomen was soft,
nontender.  Extremities showed no cyanosis, clubbing or
edema.

HOSPITAL COURSE:  The patient had complaints of slurred
speech and unsteady gait and dizziness and anorexia.
Therefore, the patient was admitted on 1974-05-13 and taken to
the operating room on 1980-04-19.  He underwent a right
temporal craniotomy for excision of this metastatic lesion.
There were no intraoperative complications.  Postoperatively,
he was awake, alert and oriented times three.  Visual fields
were full.  Extraocular movements were full.  The face was
intact.  Sensation was intact.  His strength was 09-02 in all
muscle groups and he had no drift.  He was monitored in the
recovery room overnight and he was stable and transferred to
the regular floor on postoperative day number one.  He had a
postoperative magnetic resonance imaging scan which showed
good excision of the tumor.  He was seen by physical therapy
and occupational therapy and found to be safe for discharge
to home.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:
1. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg p.o. once daily.
2. Atorvastatin 60 mg p.o. once daily.
3. Chlorpromazine 25 mg p.o. once daily p.r.n. hiccups.
4. Decadron 2 mg p.o. twice a day for four days and then
   discontinue.
5. Pantoprazole 40 mg p.o. once daily.


FOLLOW UP:  The patient will follow-up in the brain tumor
clinic on 1998-10-27, at 2:00 p.m. and follow-up with Dr.Sandra Parker
office on 1998-02-28, for staple removal.

CONDITION ON DISCHARGE:  Stable at the time of discharge.





                        [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] Dorsey, 67946
